summary: The pelvic diaphragm is formed by levator ani and coccygeus muscles; levator ani has three parts: pubococcygeus, puborectalis, and iliococcygeus.
The pelvic diaphragm is formed by the obturator internus muscle.
Levator ani has only two parts: pubococcygeus and iliococcygeus.
The puborectalis sling maintains the anorectal angle of approximately 90 degrees, which is essential for faecal continence.
The pubococcygeus maintains the anorectal angle.
The anorectal angle is maintained at 180 degrees for normal continence.
The internal iliac artery is the main arterial supply to the pelvic viscera, walls, and perineum, dividing into anterior and posterior divisions.
The external iliac artery is the main supply to pelvic viscera.
The common iliac artery directly supplies the pelvic viscera without dividing.
The gynaecoid pelvis has a rounded inlet and subpubic angle greater than 90 degrees, and is the most common type in females, ideal for childbirth.
The android pelvis is the most common type in females.
The gynaecoid pelvis has a heart-shaped inlet.
In BPH, the median lobe of the prostate enlarges and obstructs the internal urethral orifice, causing urinary retention.
In BPH, the lateral lobes obstruct the external urethral orifice.
BPH primarily affects the posterior lobe of the prostate.
The sacral plexus (L4-S4) gives rise to the sciatic nerve, the largest nerve in the body, which exits through the greater sciatic foramen.
The sacral plexus gives rise to the femoral nerve.
The sciatic nerve exits through the lesser sciatic foramen.
Uterine prolapse results from weakening of the levator ani muscle and the cardinal and uterosacral ligaments that support the uterus.
Uterine prolapse is caused by weakening of the broad ligament.
The round ligament is the primary support preventing uterine prolapse.
Suprapubic cystostomy is performed 2 cm above the pubic symphysis when the bladder is distended, as the distended bladder rises above the peritoneal reflection.
Suprapubic cystostomy is performed 5 cm above the pubic symphysis.
The peritoneum always covers the anterior surface of the bladder.
